questions.question:
"I put the brown paper in my pocket along with the chalks, and possibly other things. I suppose every one must have reflected how primeval and how poetical are the things that one carries in one's pocket: the pocket-knife, for instance the type of all human tools, the infant of the sword. Once I planned to write a book of poems entirely about the things in my pocket. But I found it would be too long: and the age of the great epics is past."
(From G.K. Chesterton's "A Piece of Chalk")

Based only on the information provided in the above passage, which one of the following statements is true?

A. The author of the passage carries a mirror in his pocket to reflect upon things.
B. The author of the passage had decided to write a poem on epics.
C. The pocket-knife is described as the infant of the sword.
D. Epics are described as too inconvenient to write.
questions.answer: C. The pocket-knife is described as the infant of the sword.
questions.to_find: Which statement is true based solely on the passage

understand.plain: The narrator reflects on the poetic nature of everyday pocket items. He singles out the pocket-knife, calling it the most primal human tool. He dreamed of writing a whole book of poems about pocket things but realized it would grow too long. The question asks which one claim is directly supported by the passage text.

keywords.explain: Belonging to the earliest stage of development; ancient and original
keywords.example: The pocket-knife is called primeval because it is one of humanity's oldest tools

keywords.explain: A metaphor meaning a smaller, early ancestor; the pocket-knife is a miniature forerunner of the sword
keywords.example: The author uses 'infant' to suggest the pocket-knife is a primitive, scaled-down version of the sword

keywords.explain: A stated fact is explicitly written in the passage; an inference goes beyond what is stated
keywords.example: The passage says 'the pocket-knife...the infant of the sword' — that is a stated fact, not an inference

understand.visual_alt: A short literary passage about pocket items with four answer options testing close reading

given.aim: Identify the statement that is directly and accurately supported by the passage

terms.meaning: The author explicitly calls the pocket-knife 'the infant of the sword'
terms.example: Direct quote: 'the pocket-knife...the infant of the sword'
terms.connects: Option C repeats this claim word-for-word, making it verifiably true

terms.meaning: The passage never mentions a mirror; 'reflected' is used figuratively ('every one must have reflected how primeval...')
terms.example: Confusing figurative 'reflected upon' with a literal mirror is a classic reading trap
terms.connects: Option A is false — it misreads the figurative use of 'reflected'

terms.meaning: The author says the age of great epics is past, not that epics are too inconvenient to write
terms.example: Passage: 'the age of the great epics is past' — this is about an era, not inconvenience
terms.connects: Option D distorts the actual claim; epics are not called 'inconvenient'

given.plan: Check each option against the exact wording of the passage; the correct answer must be something the passage explicitly states, not something inferred or distorted

steps.title: Evaluate Option A
steps.apply: Option A claims the author carries a mirror to reflect upon things. The word 'reflected' in the passage is used figuratively ('I suppose every one must have reflected how primeval...'). No mirror is mentioned. Option A is false.
steps.note: Watch for figurative vs. literal language in passage reading

steps.title: Evaluate Option B
steps.apply: Option B says the author decided to write a poem on epics. The passage says he planned to write a book of poems about things in his pocket, not about epics. Epics are only mentioned to note their era has passed. Option B is false.
steps.note: The subject of the proposed book was pocket items, not epics

steps.title: Evaluate Option C
steps.apply: Option C states: 'The pocket-knife is described as the infant of the sword.' The passage reads: 'the pocket-knife, for instance the type of all human tools, the infant of the sword.' This is a direct, verbatim match. Option C is true.
steps.note: Correct answer — exact textual evidence supports this claim

steps.title: Evaluate Option D
steps.apply: Option D says epics are described as too inconvenient to write. The passage says 'the age of the great epics is past' — meaning the era for epics has ended, not that they are inconvenient. Option D is false.
steps.note: Distortion: 'age is past' does not mean 'inconvenient to write'

solution.result: Option C is the only statement that is directly and explicitly supported by the passage text.

questions.formula_note: This is a passage reading question requiring close textual analysis; no mathematical formulas are needed.

questions.question:
Healthy eating is a critical component of healthy aging. When should one start eating healthy? It turns out that it is never too early. For example, babies who start eating healthy in the first year are more likely to have better overall health as they get older.

Which one of the following is the CORRECT logical inference based on the information in the above passage?

A. Healthy eating is important for those with good health conditions, but not for others.
B. Eating healthy can be started at any age; earlier the better.
C. Eating healthy and better overall health are more correlated at a young age, but not older age.
D. Healthy eating is more important for adults than kids.
questions.answer: B. Eating healthy can be started at any age; earlier the better.
questions.to_find: Correct logical inference from the passage about healthy eating and aging

understand.plain: The passage argues that healthy eating is vital for healthy aging and that it is never too early to begin. It backs this with the example of babies who eat well in their first year being healthier later. The key logical inference must be fully consistent with all claims in the passage.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that must follow necessarily from the information given, without adding new assumptions
keywords.example: If the passage says 'never too early', a valid inference is that any age is acceptable, with earlier being better

keywords.explain: The phrase means there is no age at which starting healthy eating is premature; the earlier, the better
keywords.example: Even babies in their first year benefit, so starting at any point — especially young — is endorsed

keywords.explain: An essential, indispensable part of a process or outcome
keywords.example: Healthy eating is not optional or conditional — it is critical for everyone's healthy aging

understand.visual_alt: Short passage about healthy eating linked to aging, with four multiple choice inference options

given.aim: Select the inference that is both true and fully supported by the passage without distortion

terms.meaning: Healthy eating is a critical component of healthy aging — applicable to all
terms.example: No restriction is placed on who benefits from healthy eating
terms.connects: Eliminates Option A (which restricts benefit only to those already in good health)

terms.meaning: 'It is never too early' — healthy eating can begin at any age, with earlier being better
terms.example: Babies in year 1 are cited as evidence that starting very early yields better outcomes

terms.meaning: The passage does not compare age groups (young vs. old) in terms of correlation or importance
terms.example: No data is given showing the effect is only at a young age or more important for adults
terms.connects: Eliminates Options C and D as they make unsupported comparative claims

given.plan: Map each option against the full passage; accept only the option that all passage statements support and none contradict

steps.title: Check Option A
steps.apply: Option A restricts healthy eating's importance to 'those with good health conditions'. The passage says healthy eating is 'critical' for all — it is a component of healthy aging for everyone, with no exclusion. Option A contradicts the passage.
steps.note: Passage is universal — no conditional based on current health status

steps.title: Check Option B
steps.apply: Option B: 'Eating healthy can be started at any age; earlier the better.' The passage states 'it is never too early' and shows babies starting healthy eating in year 1 do better. This exactly matches — any age is fine, and starting earlier yields better health. Option B is fully supported.
steps.note: This is the correct inference — it captures both 'never too early' and the graduated benefit

steps.title: Check Option C
steps.apply: Option C claims the correlation between healthy eating and better health applies at young age but NOT at older age. The passage mentions babies as an example but never says the benefit disappears with age. This is an unsupported restriction. Option C is incorrect.
steps.note: The passage does not limit the effect to childhood

steps.title: Check Option D
steps.apply: Option D says healthy eating is more important for adults than kids. The passage actually emphasises early (even infant-stage) healthy eating and never ranks importance by adult vs. child. Option D contradicts the passage's emphasis on early-life eating. Option D is incorrect.
steps.note: Passage prioritises early start, not adult importance

solution.result: Option B is the correct inference: healthy eating can start at any age, and starting earlier is better.

questions.formula_note: This is a passage-based logical inference question requiring reading comprehension skills; no mathematical formulas are involved.

questions.question:
In the last few years, several new shopping malls were opened in the city. The total number of visitors in the malls is impressive. However, the total revenue generated through sales in the shops in these malls is generally low.

Which one of the following is the CORRECT logical inference based on the information in the above passage?

A. Fewer people are visiting the malls but spending more.
B. More people are visiting the malls but not spending enough.
C. More people are visiting the malls and spending more.
D. Fewer people are visiting the malls and not spending enough.
questions.answer: D. Fewer people are visiting the malls and not spending enough.
questions.to_find: Correct logical inference about mall visitors and spending from the passage

understand.plain: The passage presents a contrast: visitor numbers are impressive (high) but revenue from sales is generally low. This gap between high footfall and low revenue is the key fact to reason from. The correct inference must reconcile both facts simultaneously.

keywords.explain: The passage explicitly says the total number of visitors is impressive, meaning the count is large or noteworthy
keywords.example: High footfall: many people are entering and walking around the malls

keywords.explain: Despite many visitors, the money earned through actual purchases in shops is generally low
keywords.example: Visitors browse but do not buy enough — this disconnect between traffic and sales is central

keywords.explain: The conclusion that must follow from combining the two facts: high visitors AND low revenue
keywords.example: If visitors are high but revenue is low, people are visiting but not spending sufficiently

understand.visual_alt: Passage contrasting high mall visitor numbers with low sales revenue, with four inference options

given.aim: Identify the inference consistent with BOTH facts: impressive visitor count AND low revenue

terms.meaning: Many people are visiting the malls (the total number is 'impressive')
terms.example: This rules out any option claiming 'fewer people are visiting'
terms.connects: Eliminates Options A and D which say 'fewer people are visiting'

terms.meaning: Sales revenue is generally low despite the large visitor count
terms.example: Visitors are not converting into buyers, or are not spending enough per visit
terms.connects: Eliminates Option C which says 'spending more'

terms.meaning: Many visitors + low revenue = people visit but do not spend enough
terms.example: Option B captures exactly this: more people visiting but not spending enough
terms.connects: Supports Option B as the correct inference

given.plan: The answer must satisfy: visitor count is high AND revenue is low. Only Option B matches both conditions.

steps.title: Identify the two key facts
steps.apply: Fact 1: 'The total number of visitors in the malls is impressive' => visitor count is HIGH. Fact 2: 'the total revenue generated through sales is generally low' => spending/revenue is LOW. Any correct inference must preserve both facts.
steps.note: Both facts must be captured simultaneously

steps.title: Eliminate options inconsistent with high visitor count
steps.apply: Options A and D say 'Fewer people are visiting'. The passage says visitor numbers are impressive (high), so both A and D contradict Fact 1 and are eliminated.

steps.title: Eliminate options inconsistent with low revenue
steps.apply: Option C says 'spending more', which would mean high revenue — but the passage says revenue is low. Option C contradicts Fact 2 and is eliminated.
steps.note: High spending would produce high revenue, not low

steps.title: Confirm Option B
steps.apply: Option B: 'More people are visiting the malls but not spending enough.' This aligns with Fact 1 (more visitors) and Fact 2 (low revenue because visitors don't spend enough). Option B is the correct inference.
steps.note: Official answer key records this as D, but logical analysis from the passage text confirms Option B. Verify with official source.

solution.result: The correct logical inference is: More people are visiting the malls but not spending enough (Option B). Note: the official answer key records D; however, Option D contradicts the passage's statement that visitor numbers are impressive.

questions.formula_note: This is a logical inference from a short passage; no mathematical formulas are used. The answer key records D, but passage analysis strongly supports B — the passage explicitly states visitor numbers are impressive (high), ruling out 'fewer people visiting'.

questions.question:
The James Webb telescope, recently launched in space, is giving humankind unprecedented access to the depths of time by imaging very old stars formed almost 13 billion years ago. Astrophysicists and cosmologists believe that this odyssey in space may even shed light on the existence of dark matter. Dark matter is supposed to interact only via the electromagnetic, the weak, or the strong interaction. This may justify the epithet "dark" in dark matter.

Based only on the above paragraph, which one of the following statements is FALSE?

A. No other telescope has captured images of stars older than those captured by the James Webb telescope.
B. People other than astrophysicists and cosmologists may also believe in the existence of dark matter.
C. The James Webb telescope could be of use in the research on dark matter.
D. Dark matter interacts via the gravitational interaction.
questions.answer: D. Dark matter interacts via the gravitational interaction.
questions.to_find: Which statement is FALSE based solely on the passage

understand.plain: The passage describes the James Webb Space Telescope's capability to image very ancient stars and the hope that it may help understand dark matter. It specifically states that dark matter is supposed to interact only via the electromagnetic, weak, or strong interaction. The question asks which option is FALSE — meaning which claim is directly contradicted or unsupported by the passage.

keywords.explain: According to the passage, dark matter interacts only via electromagnetic, weak, or strong interactions — gravitational interaction is not mentioned
keywords.example: The passage lists three interactions for dark matter; the absence of gravitational interaction from this list makes Option D false

keywords.explain: No telescope before has provided such deep time-imaging capability, implying Webb images older stars than any previous telescope
keywords.example: Option A says no other telescope captured older stars — this is supported by 'unprecedented'

keywords.explain: A cautious expression meaning it is possible (not certain) that Webb could help explain dark matter
keywords.example: Option C (Webb could be useful for dark matter research) is supported by this phrase

understand.visual_alt: Passage about James Webb telescope imaging ancient stars and possibly illuminating dark matter existence

given.aim: Find the statement that is directly contradicted by or absent from the passage

terms.meaning: Dark matter interacts only via electromagnetic, weak, or strong interaction — gravitational interaction is NOT mentioned
terms.example: Option D claims dark matter interacts gravitationally; the passage contradicts this by listing only three other interactions
terms.connects: Makes Option D the FALSE statement

terms.meaning: The passage calls Webb's access 'unprecedented', implying no other telescope has imaged equally old stars
terms.example: Option A is consistent with 'unprecedented' — it is TRUE per the passage
terms.connects: Option A is not the false statement

terms.meaning: The passage says astrophysicists and cosmologists believe dark matter exists; it does not restrict belief only to them
terms.example: Others could also believe in dark matter — Option B is a plausible inference and is TRUE (not provably false)
terms.connects: Option B is not the false statement

given.plan: Check each option against the passage; the false one either contradicts an explicit claim or asserts something the passage explicitly rules out

steps.title: Evaluate Option A
steps.apply: Option A: 'No other telescope has captured images of stars older than those captured by the James Webb telescope.' The passage says Webb gives 'unprecedented access' to imaging stars 13 billion years old. 'Unprecedented' means no prior telescope matched this. Option A is TRUE (not false).
steps.note: 'Unprecedented' directly supports Option A being true

steps.title: Evaluate Option B
steps.apply: Option B: 'People other than astrophysicists and cosmologists may also believe in the existence of dark matter.' The passage only mentions astrophysicists and cosmologists believing, but does not say ONLY they believe. Option B is a reasonable inference and is TRUE (not false based on the passage).
steps.note: The passage doesn't restrict belief to those two groups

steps.title: Evaluate Option C
steps.apply: Option C: 'The James Webb telescope could be of use in the research on dark matter.' The passage says Webb 'may even shed light on the existence of dark matter.' This directly supports Option C. Option C is TRUE (not false).
steps.note: 'May even shed light on' = could be useful for dark matter research

steps.title: Evaluate Option D
steps.apply: Option D: 'Dark matter interacts via the gravitational interaction.' The passage states dark matter interacts 'only via the electromagnetic, the weak, or the strong interaction.' Gravitational interaction is explicitly excluded by the word 'only' limiting interactions to the three listed. Option D is FALSE — it contradicts the passage.
steps.note: The word 'only' in the passage rules out gravitational interaction for dark matter

solution.result: Option D is FALSE: the passage explicitly states dark matter interacts only via electromagnetic, weak, or strong interactions, not gravitational interaction.

questions.formula_note: This is a passage comprehension question testing identification of a false statement; no mathematical formulas are required.
